releases.title: Reach out
releases.summary: Your mail now reaches Gmail, Microsoft 365, and Fastmail, and your automations react to the outside world — an incoming webhook, a file changing on disk, or the app starting up.

text.text: for full send and sync, not just IMAP. Add an account, sign in, and your mail syncs both ways.
— a workflow can run whenever something
. Wire up a form, a service, or a script to kick off an automation.
on disk, or once each time the app starts.
no longer fails on hidden housekeeping files.
— the number keycaps, ©, and ® show up again instead of blank tiles.
— every glance widget's toolbar lines up on one baseline.

releases.title: Your inbox starts working for you
releases.summary: Email can trigger automations and get AI-triaged, attachments are first-class files you can search and link, and the assistant can hand a reply straight to your composer.

text.text: — a new message can now fire an
, with an AI-triage step that reads the mail and decides what to do. Route, label, or act on your inbox without lifting a finger.
you can search, link, and reuse. The reading pane shows attachment chips; click one to fetch and open it in place.
— reply and forward preserve the original's formatting with HTML-aware quoting.
— hand an assistant reply straight to the
— the on-device model downloads only when you
shows the app name in its header when nothing is open.
now offers a clear next step instead of a dead shield.

body.text: Plus a sweep of dogfood fixes: new objects rename in place, empty states tell the truth, image figures stay in the content column, tidier bookmark cards, cleaner Anytype imports, and clearer reconnect prompts.

text.text: Brainstorm's building blocks are now on npm under
— tokens, types, the React‑Yjs hooks, Block Protocol, the SDK, and the editor.

releases.title: Your whole mail history
releases.summary: Load older mail on demand — the synced window extends in place, page by page, and fixing a broken account no longer means removing it.

text.text: — a button at the bottom of the list walks every folder deeper into history, past the sync window you chose. Your position is remembered, so each press resumes where the last one stopped — all the way back to your oldest mail.
reveals more), so thousands of messages stay fast.
opens pre-filled with its stored settings: type the new password and you're back, keeping all synced mail. The sidebar's account menu also gains

releases.title: Mailbox grows up
releases.summary: The Mailbox becomes a real mail client — rich HTML compose, visible and removable accounts, working remote images, your choice of sync history — and the built-in Browser stops tripping login anti-bot walls.

text.text: — the message body is now a real editor (
) and mail sends as proper HTML with a plain-text fallback.
— every account shows in the sidebar with
actions; a failed sync shows a readable error with a
button instead of a dead end.
One click to open a mail
— single messages no longer pretend to be conversations; folder names drop the
server prefix; blocked remote images stop painting broken icons.
— the opt-in was silently blocked by a second security policy; images actually appear.
— connecting an IMAP account now asks how far back to sync (
), and a flaky mail server can no longer crash the sync engine.
— the built-in Browser now presents a standard Chrome identity, so anti-bot walls (X.com and friends) stop rejecting sign-ins and posts.
— corner radii come from tokens everywhere, hero buttons share one enforced size, and disabled buttons keep their finish.
— layout routing and image widths carry over; big imports run in the background without blocking the app.

releases.title: Imports made whole
releases.summary: The Anytype import now lands every file — pasted screenshots included, none swapped — and the rough edges around it are gone: gallery tiles render properly, imported notes recover their titles, menus fit their labels, and IMAP setup stops eating app passwords.

text.text: — the Anytype importer now reads the export's own file map, so all attachments bind exactly: name-less pasted screenshots import instead of showing broken images, and screenshots taken in the same second no longer swap content. Re-running an import repairs an existing vault in place.
— imported notes that opened with an empty title now adopt their name on first open.
— Gallery and Grid tiles fill their cells edge to edge, image files show real thumbnails, and untitled items sort to the end instead of walling the top of the view.

— connecting an IMAP account trims pasted app passwords (a stray trailing space read as wrong credentials), a failed sync now says what to check, and the connect dialog wears the shared segmented controls.
— nested bullet lists in answers render as real lists instead of inline dashes.
— option dropdowns are wide enough for their labels, with the checkmark column aligned; no more “G…” guessing games.
— AI provider names wrap to two lines instead of truncating mid-word.

releases.title: A new coat of indigo
releases.summary: Brainstorm drops the pink: Default Light and Default Dark are now the out-of-box themes, with a new violet-indigo app icon, a fresh default wallpaper, and a matching welcome screen. Plus a round of Settings polish.

text.text: — fresh vaults now open in Default Light (neutral, violet-indigo accent) and toggle into Default Dark. Rose and Midnight stay in the theme catalog — your current choice is untouched.
— the bolt now sits on a violet-indigo tile, in your dock, tray, and installer.
— a new bundled low-poly landscape is the light-mode default for new vaults, and joins the wallpaper catalog alongside rose-mountains and stormy-sea.
— the welcome screen now opens on the green-valley scene in Default Light.

— disabled buttons keep readable labels on light themes, the avatar shows a camera glyph until you have a name or photo, empty trusted-site entries can't be added, proxy details no longer repeat themselves, and the search-index stats tiles align in a clean grid.
— installed-app lists (notifications, icon picker) no longer shuffle their order between visits.
— one themed ring everywhere, correctly scaled at every size.

releases.title: Your files land, your numbers roll up
releases.summary: Anytype imports now bring the actual files into your documents — and Database learns rollups, the Agent can write into notes, and every drag has a keyboard twin.

text.text: — a new column kind that aggregates across a relation (count, sum, average, min, max) — and computed columns now show on cards and lists, not just the grid.
— an "Add to note" button on Agent replies inserts the answer into a note you pick (or links the chat), with provenance both ways. Only you can trigger it — never the model on its own.
— Move to folder, Link to note, Move to column, Move to date: every drag-and-drop now has a menu + shortcut twin, announced to screen readers.
— images render inside your imported documents and PDFs link to their files (an import that found 0 of 457 files now lands all the export carries).
— running the same import twice no longer duplicates note bodies or grows your vault by hundreds of MB, and a re-export under Anytype's new filename updates the same objects.
— tag values show in the Properties panel and imported notes open with their title in the editor.
— links between your own objects and files no longer render as dead anchors.
If you're on 0.5.0, the update toast will bring 0.5.1 to you — or grab it in

releases.title: Switch in, stay current
releases.summary: Bring your whole Anytype space with you — pages, properties, tags, collections and links arrive as native objects — and Brainstorm now finds its own updates and offers them as a click.

text.text: — Settings → Backup & Migration accepts an Anytype export (folder or zip). Pages arrive with their full editor structure — headings, lists, checkboxes, links, images — plus their original dates; relations become properties, tags stay tags, collections become Lists, and linked objects stay linked. Importing the same export again updates instead of duplicating.
— Brainstorm checks for new versions in the background and shows a toast with a Download button when one is available; one more click restarts into it.

— Contacts finds people who appear more than once (same email or name) and merges them into one card, keeping every email, phone and link. Merged-away duplicates go to the Bin, so a merge is reversible.
— the Journal and Tasks editors now offer the same embed and transclusion blocks as Notes.
— a finished import explains anything it skipped and offers “Import another…” right there.
— pinned app tiles now always show the app's current name.
— gallery tiles lost their leftover boxes, and a new guard keeps every Files view frame-free for good.
— a widget that ended up off-screen (an old resize bug) now pulls itself back onto the dashboard.
— big imports parse once, read only the files they need, and no longer slow down as your vault grows.
— text fields across the entire app now sit on the shared control scale.
— and from now on, Brainstorm will tell you when the next one is ready.

releases.title: Fixes across the desktop
releases.summary: A patch release: editors outside Notes get their styles back, the Files grid stops overlapping tiles, reminders open what they are about, and four apps get shorter names.

text.text: — Tasks, Journal, Chat and other editor hosts had drifted from Notes: callouts lost their tone icon but kept its empty gutter, the inline toolbar stacked vertically, titles were unstyled, embed cards oversized. The shared editor theme now matches Notes.
— resizing the window or changing the tile size no longer paints tiles over each other, and tiles drop the permanent outline — hover and selection still tint them.
— a widget with a toolbar no longer pushes its empty-state message below centre.
— analytics only runs in packaged builds, so dev, CI and test runs stop registering as new users.
— clicking a Calendar or Tasks reminder, in the notification center or the system popup, jumps straight to the event or task.
— the dashboard shows its alignment grid while you drag an icon, so drops land where you expect.
— Form Designer, Theme Editor, Web Browser and Code Editor are now Forms, Themes, Browser and Code.

releases.title: Analytics that actually reaches us
releases.summary: Beta usage analytics can talk to Amplitude again, using one anonymous install id and without sending vault ids or other identity.

body.text: After 0.4.3 turned on beta analytics, the shell and every app blocked Amplitude with a tight Content Security Policy. Events never left the device, and the console filled with CSP errors.

text.text: — shell and all first-party apps allow Amplitude EU hosts for connect, the engagement CDN script, and blob workers used by the SDK.
— every renderer shares one stable per-install id as Amplitude's device id. Not your vault, email, or device key.
— vault ids are no longer attached to events; the API key stays private to the analytics module; IP reverse-geolocation is off.
Analytics runs on pre-1.0 builds only and is disclosed on first launch and under

releases.title: Release pipeline unblocked
releases.summary: Fixes the Files and Graph typecheck failures that kept CI red since 0.4.3, and caps Rust parallel jobs on release runners so arm64 packaging no longer OOMs mid-build.

body.text: The 0.4.3 cut never fully published: CI typecheck failed on Files and Graph, and the Linux arm64 release runner dropped mid-build. This patch lands the type fixes and hardens the release workflow so packaging can finish.

text.text: — plurals go through a catalog-bound helper so the typed translator typechecks cleanly.
— remote-presence paint is wired through app state (the animation loop no longer references an out-of-scope binding).
on the package matrix so arm64 hosts stay under memory pressure while linking native addons.
If you already had the draft analytics build, this is the first complete multi-platform publish of that work.

releases.title: Beta usage analytics
releases.summary: During the public beta, Brainstorm now collects anonymous product analytics so we can see which features people use and where they get stuck — with a clear disclosure on first launch and in Settings → Privacy.

body.text: Brainstorm is still in public beta. To improve the product we need a picture of real usage — which apps people open, where the shell feels confusing, and which flows succeed. This patch turns on anonymous analytics for beta builds only.

text.text: — vault opened, app launched, launcher and settings opened, and similar shell-level actions.
No vault contents, passwords, or note text
— anonymized recordings of how you navigate the UI during beta, hosted in the
, so we can spot friction in real sessions.
popover explains the policy. Dismiss it with
; it won't appear again on this device.
— a standing notice while we're in beta, with the same explanation.

body.text: Analytics runs only on pre-1.0 beta builds. When Brainstorm reaches version 1.0, collection turns off automatically and these notices go away.

releases.title: Presence on canvas
releases.summary: Graph now shows live collaborator cursors and selection rings on shared boards; Database joins the presence fleet. Twelve apps ship Spanish UI overlays when you switch language in Settings.

text.text: — on a shared graph, you now see collaborators'
move live on the canvas, the same way Whiteboard already worked.
when others are viewing the same record, completing the fleet-wide presence rollout.
now updates chrome in twelve apps live (no restart): Code editor, Browser, Preview, Chat, Agent, Contacts, Mailbox, Books, Theme editor, Form designer, Automations, and Files.

releases.title: Presence everywhere
releases.summary: Live presence avatars now show in Tasks, Chat, Journal, Calendar events, Graph, Code editor, Preview, and Books — plus browser tab fixes and Settings polish.

text.text: now appear in every shared-entity app header: Tasks, Chat, Journal, Calendar (when editing an event), Graph, Code editor, Preview, and Books — not just Notes and Whiteboard.
— tab switching no longer leaves a blank view or bleeds content between tabs.
— Identity section shows a copyable fingerprint chip; secondary text is easier to read; Updates card layout tidied.

releases.title: See who's here
releases.summary: Live presence — when you share a board or a note, you now see your collaborators' cursors and avatars in real time. Plus a per-site trust switch for the browser and an instant light/dark shell.

text.text: — everyone on a shared board sees each other's
in the header shows who else is viewing a note with you.

body.text: Presence is display-only and end-to-end encrypted like everything else — it rides the same sealed sync channel and grants no access on its own.

text.text: (Settings → Privacy) lets login-gated sites like x.com work normally, while every other site keeps the strict tracker- and cookie-blocking default.
in lockstep with your open apps, instead of lagging a beat behind.

releases.title: Reminders that don't slip through
releases.summary: A task or event reminder now fires even if the app wasn't running when it came due — it catches up the next time you open Brainstorm. Plus a fix for list numbers going missing.

text.text: — if a reminder came due while Brainstorm was
, it now catches up and notifies you once the next time you open the app, instead of being silently missed.
show their markers again in comment and chat composers.

releases.title: Removing someone really removes them
releases.summary: When you take a collaborator off a shared note, page, or channel, they can no longer read anything you write afterward — access removal is now enforced by encryption, not just policy. Plus a PDF crash fix.

text.text: from a shared item, its encryption key is rotated and re-sealed for the people who remain. The removed person is left holding only the old key, so every edit you make
they're removed is unreadable to them — even if their app is still connected.
— remove someone while you're offline and the key rotation is queued, then completes automatically the moment you reconnect.
What they already saw, they keep.
Like every honest end-to-end encrypted system, removal is forward-looking: it stops future access — it can't un-send what already synced to their device.

releases.title: Sharing polish
releases.summary: Small fixes on the back of the sharing release: your own name shows up instead of "Anonymous", a couple of rough edges smoothed, and a fix for a harmless console error in Tasks.

text.text: — comments you post and your row in a shared item's member list now show your
; until you do, it falls back to your key fingerprint.
in the invite-code field now adds the collaborator, no click needed.
— renaming a task no longer logs a harmless console error on blur.

releases.title: Share your work
releases.summary: Invite teammates to any note or collection, give them Editor or Viewer access, and edit together — changes sync live, end-to-end encrypted, with the relay never seeing your content. Viewers are now truly read-only.

text.text: any note or collection from its
— add a teammate by their invite code or by name, and see everyone with access at a glance.
(read-only) access, and revoke it any time. A Viewer's edits are now blocked at the data layer, not just hidden — so read-only means read-only.
— once shared, edits flow both ways in real time, converging cleanly even after you've both been offline.
Everything is encrypted per-entity on your devices; the sync relay only ever sees ciphertext, never your content or who you're working with.

One note on removing access: revoking a teammate stops future grants and hides the entity from them, but does not yet rotate the encryption key. A removed collaborator who already has the key can still read edits made after they're removed. Full rotate-on-revoke (new key, re-shared only with remaining members) is coming in a follow-up.

releases.title: Reminders you can rely on
releases.summary: A quality release: task and calendar reminders now fire even when the app is closed, duplicate alerts are gone, synced attachments can be cleaned up server-side, and every known dependency vulnerability is patched.

text.text: — due dates, scheduled starts, and event reminders now fire
even when the app is closed
. Until now an alert only fired while its app window was open.

body.text: Opening the app no longer risks a duplicate — the shell and the app coordinate so each alert appears exactly once.
Alerts respect Do Not Disturb and per-app mutes, and land in the notification center like everything else.

text.text: — your devices now report which encrypted attachments they still use, so the sync node can safely reclaim storage from deleted files. Reclamation stays conservative: anything a recently-seen device references is kept.
— all 69 known dependency vulnerabilities patched, including the mail sender and the spreadsheet preview parser; the build toolchain moved to current majors.

releases.title: Search that understands meaning
releases.summary: Semantic search arrives: an on-device model finds your notes by meaning, not just keywords. Plus a background-activity center, six new dashboard widgets, a richer chat composer with @-mentions, and editor templates.

text.text: — “vacation ideas” surfaces the note titled “places to visit this summer”. The model runs entirely

body.text: The model downloads once on first run, with a progress indicator — search works normally in the meantime.
Indexing is incremental: edits re-index only what changed, so big vaults stay fast.

text.text: shows background work (downloads, indexing, sync) in one place.
— six new dashboard widgets, size presets in the widget menu, and keyboard
— a richer composer with formatting and inline
from the slash menu, and save any document as a template.
— captured articles now keep their images, stored encrypted in your vault.
— a fresh onboarding look with the new
— accent-colored and muted text now meet

releases.title: A full pass over every app
releases.summary: A fixes release on the public beta, from a sweep across every app: status cells edit as a pick list, the calendar and preview render cleanly, chat messages stay out of the file browsers, and the assistant shows real titles instead of internal ids.

text.text: — status and select cells edit as a
: pick a value already in the column, or type a new one.
— multi-day events draw as one clean bar in month view instead of a clipped, repeated title in every day.
— centers its empty state and docks the details panel on the right, like every other app.
— chat messages no longer flood the vault browsers as untitled rows.
— renders the documents it cites as clickable titles instead of raw ids.

body.text: Plus tidier Agent settings, clearer empty states in Automations and Contacts, and task date chips that read correctly to screen readers.

releases.title: Pre-alpha development build
releases.summary: The very first cut of Brainstorm — a desktop knowledge OS with first-party apps for notes, tasks, files, code, journaling, whiteboards, bookmarks and more. Sandboxed, local-first, end-to-end encrypted at rest.

body.text: Thirteen first-party apps land in this build. Each one runs in its own sandboxed renderer, talks to the shell through the capability broker, and shares a single design system.

text.text: — six view kinds: grid, list, gallery, board, calendar, timeline.
— multi-subject pattern filters with edge-accurate history animation.
BlockEmbed renders sandboxed block frames in an isolated iframe with a typed
transport so third-party blocks never touch the host directly.
through a capability-gated service, with chunked uploads for large files.
Per-scheme and per-extension default pinning under
option and a consent prompt for first-time OS handoff.
), shared object menu with Pin / Unpin / Open / Remove, and a unified mention typeahead.

body.text: At-rest encryption per vault, sync de-risk spike, per-entity data keys generated on create, and an on-disk encryption flip with a startup contract probe. Your data never leaves the device without you knowing.

text.text: The vault format may still change before 0.1.0. Keep a backup of anything you put in here.
